critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the Los Angeles Aqueduct, tracing its history, engineering, and environmental and social impacts. It highlights the aqueduct's role in supplying water to Los Angeles, the controversies surrounding its construction and effects on Owens Valley, and the ongoing challenges posed by climate variability and environmental concerns.
mainPoints: The Los Angeles Aqueduct was completed in 1913, bringing water from the Owens River in the Eastern Sierra to Los Angeles, enabling the city's growth.
The aqueduct is a gravity-fed system spanning about 300 miles, consisting of canals, tunnels, pipes, and reservoirs.
The diversion of water caused significant ecological damage and social conflict in Owens Valley, including the California Water Wars and the drying of Owens Lake.
The system includes two aqueducts built at different times, with infrastructure such as reservoirs, tunnels, and hydroelectric plants to manage flow and generate power.
The St. Francis Dam failure in 1928 was a major disaster linked to the aqueduct system, killing over 400 people and damaging the project's reputation.
Environmental regulations and lawsuits have forced reductions in water diversion from sources like Mono Lake, reflecting ongoing ecological concerns.
Climate change and variable snowmelt patterns are creating uncertainty about the aqueduct's future water supply reliability.
The aqueduct exemplifies the complex interplay of engineering, environmental impact, politics, and social consequences in large infrastructure projects.
critique.truthfulness: The article is largely accurate and well-supported by historical facts and engineering details. It correctly describes the construction timeline, engineering features, and the socio-environmental consequences of the aqueduct, including the California Water Wars and the St. Francis Dam disaster. The information about the aqueduct's gravity-fed design, the role of reservoirs, and the challenges of water management aligns with established knowledge. The discussion of environmental impacts, such as Owens Lake dust pollution and Mono Lake diversions, is consistent with documented issues. The article also fairly notes the complexity and controversy surrounding the project, reflecting multiple perspectives. However, it is a single-source narrative from a specialized engineering blog and does not cite multiple independent sources or perspectives, which limits its classification as fully reliable journalism.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore the perspectives of Native American communities affected by the aqueduct beyond a brief mention.
It does not provide detailed citations or references to primary historical documents or academic studies.
The long-term legal and political negotiations over water rights and environmental mitigation are summarized but not deeply analyzed.
The article focuses on engineering and historical narrative but less on current water policy debates or alternative water sources for Los Angeles.
Potential impacts of climate change on future water availability are mentioned but not quantified or supported by climate data.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around California's water infrastructure, particularly the Los Angeles Aqueduct, with commenters sharing personal anecdotes, concerns about water conservation, and suggestions like desalination. There is also interest in the historical and engineering aspects of the aqueduct and related water controversies.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
The norcal/socal divide caused by the river is funny to me. I grew up in LA, then moved to the Bay Area for college. In LA we never really talked about where our water comes from. But we were always 'in a drought' and always taught to conserve water.

My wife grew up in the Bay Area, and was told the same.

But her family is from Sacramento. Up until about 15 years ago, everyone in Sacramento paid the same for water (based on square footage of your home). There were no water meters. So they didn't conserve. They ran the sprinklers in 100 degree heat for hours, they washed sidewalks with water instead sweeping, and all the other things.

But when the meters came, her Uncle blamed SoCal for "stealing his water". He complained every month when the bill came about how he has to pay more now because of SoCal.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced personal perspective on regional water usage and attitudes, reflecting real socio-political tensions around water in California.

comments.author: notepad0x90
comments.text:
Looking at what Tehran is facing (not related to the war, water shortage), I'm wondering why california isn't investing in more desalination for SoCal, especially for LA.

I see some here:

https://lynceans.org/all-posts/status-of-desalination-plants...

But there are only a few in SoCal and they're for smaller communities like carlsbad or santa barbara. So it is there and it is working for some, why not more? naturally i assume it's because everything costs more at the coast.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about desalination as a potential solution, supported by examples of existing plants, though cost and environmental factors are complex.

comments.author: strongpigeon
comments.text: Sometimes it feels like the US has lost its appetite for grand structural projects like that. Maybe it’s just that I’m unaware of them and that impression is the result of survival bias, but given how impossibly hard it is to just build anything where I live (Seattle), I’m not so sure.
comments.analysis: Insightful commentary on modern infrastructure challenges in the US, reflecting broader issues with regulatory and political hurdles.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text: Am curious, how have the shade balls been working out in terms of maintenance, coating wear, leeched microplastics, containment (so they don't wind up in oceans), etc?  In retrospect, did it make sense compared to the $250M or so uplift it would have taken to build a conventional roof?
comments.analysis: Asks a pertinent question about the environmental and economic trade-offs of shade balls, showing critical engagement with water conservation methods.

comments.author: kyledrake
comments.text: I was in Owens River Gorge last week, it's a very interesting place. It has some of the tallest single pitch rock climbing in the world, sometimes requiring 80M ropes: https://www.mountainproject.com/area/105843226/owens-river-g...
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant personal experience related to the aqueduct's geography, adding a recreational perspective though less focused on water issues.

comments.author: rimunroe
comments.text: I was surprised to find out it was largely uncovered, though I guess it probably makes it much cheaper to construct. I usually think of aqueducts as pipes or tunnels, like Persian qanāts. I wonder how much water is lost due to evaporation.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid technical observation about aqueduct design and potential water loss, reflecting curiosity about engineering trade-offs.

comments.author: scoofy
comments.text:
Just a quick plug for Cadillac Desert. People don't realize how fragile the urban areas of Coastal California are. We should.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cadillac_Desert
comments.analysis: Recommends a well-regarded resource on California water issues, adding historical context to the discussion.

comments.author: hparadiz
comments.text: The California aquaduct system is an engineering marvel.
comments.analysis: A concise statement acknowledging the aqueduct’s engineering significance, though lacking elaboration.

comments.author: TheGrassyKnoll
comments.text: Some say the LA aqueduct saved Owens Valley from development. (I’m sure the old timers out there would have a different opinion)
comments.analysis: Acknowledges differing perspectives on the aqueduct’s impact, hinting at historical controversies around water diversion.

comments.author: babblingfish
comments.text: I really dig the editorial viewpoint of this article. New journalism style meets fun facts about engineering.
comments.analysis: Expresses a positive subjective reaction to the article’s style rather than contributing factual content.
